_note: Per-specialist case material for Customer Experience (CX). Used at workerNode (per specialty turn), not at recommendation. The specialist retrieves 2-3 cases whose `business_type_category` matches the inferred category, and weaves the gap-and-feeling insight into the turn without naming the source — per CLAUDE.md GR#6 ("use the case, don't cite it"). Every case here exhibits CX's domain discipline: name the specific moment in the customer journey where the business's assumption and the customer's reality diverge, AND what the customer is probably feeling at that moment. A case that names a moment without a feeling is a process-step note; a case that names a feeling without a moment is therapy. CX owns the pairing.

cases.challenge_pattern: post-signing silence — the moment between the contract signing and the first substantive work where nothing from the business reassures the customer they chose right
cases.observation: Management consultancy with a 60-day sales cycle and a three-week gap between signed engagement letter and kickoff meeting. Close rate was strong; referral rate was mediocre. Owner assumed the referral problem was a 'post-engagement advocacy' question. The real moment was the three weeks after signing. New clients sat with buyer's remorse — they had just committed $30-60K and nothing from the consultancy had landed in their inbox except the signed contract and an invoice. By the time the kickoff happened, the client had already rehearsed their doubts to their board, their spouse, and themselves. They weren't confident advocates of the choice they'd made; they were people looking for reassurance the consultancy hadn't thought to provide.
cases.what_worked: A three-email sequence in the signing-to-kickoff window: (1) day after signing — named one specific thing the consultancy had noticed about the client's market that would shape the kickoff, proving the consultancy had already started thinking; (2) day 7 — introduced the exact team by name with a one-line note on each person's relevant background, anchoring who would be in the room; (3) day 14 — one-page summary of the three questions the kickoff would answer, so the client arrived having already started thinking about the answers. Referral rate doubled over the next two quarters. The referrals weren't the target of the intervention — they were the byproduct of clients leaving the first month feeling like they'd chosen well.
cases.what_wasted_money: Eight months of a referral-incentive program (10% credit for successful referrals) that generated almost no referrals. The incentive was addressing the wrong moment — clients who weren't confident in their own choice didn't refer friends regardless of the incentive.
cases.one_line_lesson: The moment after the customer commits and before the work begins is where buyer's remorse is born. Silence in that window is a feeling, not an absence — and the feeling is costing you the referrals you never got.

cases.challenge_pattern: handoff moment where the intake experience doesn't match the service experience — the trust built by an excellent intake call is broken when the senior person shows up differently
cases.observation: Boutique law firm where the managing partner personally took every intake call — warm, specific, remembered every detail. Conversion on intake calls was 70%+. Retention through to case conclusion was materially worse. The handoff moment was where trust broke: after the partner's warm intake, clients were transferred to an associate who communicated formally, didn't know the personal details the partner had learned, and scheduled on their own calendar. Clients interpreted the shift as a bait-and-switch. It wasn't — the associate was genuinely handling the case and the partner was supervising — but nothing about the handoff communicated continuity.
cases.what_worked: A ten-minute three-way call with the partner, the client, and the associate — the partner briefly naming two specific things they'd heard from the client that the associate would carry forward; the associate confirming by repeating those back; the partner naming what they would personally review and when. The handoff went from feeling like a demotion to feeling like a transition. Retention through case conclusion rose notably in the first year, and NPS at closing moved from neutral to strongly positive.
cases.what_wasted_money: Fourteen months of debates about whether the partner should take fewer intakes. The answer to the real problem wasn't fewer intakes — it was better handoffs. The intake quality was the firm's strongest asset; the gap was that the handoff was throwing it away.
cases.one_line_lesson: When the first experience is personal and the second is procedural, the customer feels the drop — even if the quality of work is unchanged. The handoff is where the promise made at intake either holds or breaks.

cases.challenge_pattern: call-back gap — the voicemail left after hours that doesn't get returned until the next morning, by which time the customer has already called the next contractor
cases.observation: Two-truck HVAC company with a 24-hour 'we answer every call' message and a strict 9am-5pm phone policy. After-hours calls went to voicemail with a reassuring message. The owner believed customers understood — 'we can't be up all night answering calls.' The gap was that 60% of emergency HVAC calls came in between 6pm and 11pm — a person standing in a hot kitchen in July with a broken AC, children upstairs unable to sleep. That person did not calmly leave a voicemail and wait. They called the next number on the Google listing. By 9am, they had an appointment with a competitor. The voicemail the owner returned at 9am was a corpse.
cases.what_worked: A call-forwarding arrangement to a 24/7 answering service with two rules: (1) the service texted the on-call tech within five minutes for any emergency-category call; (2) the caller received a text within ten minutes confirming their call was received and whether a tech was en route or would call back first thing. The service cost $200/month. It recovered an estimated $15-25K/month of jobs that had been silently walking to competitors. The technology was minor; the moment it addressed — the silence a panicked customer faces at 8pm — was the entire problem.
cases.what_wasted_money: Three years of ad spend bringing after-hours callers to a phone that wouldn't be answered until morning. The ads were working; the experience was throwing the results away.
cases.one_line_lesson: When a customer calls you in distress and nobody picks up, they don't tell you they're frustrated — they call the next contractor. You lose them in the silence between their emergency and your business hours.

cases.challenge_pattern: post-job silence — the moment the tech drives away and the customer has no way of knowing if the work was done well, with nothing from the business to confirm it
cases.observation: Family-owned landscaping firm with excellent technical work and a mediocre review profile. Owner assumed reviews were slow because customers were lazy. The gap was that the moment the last truck pulled out of the driveway, the customer had no framework for evaluating what they'd just received. They could see the lawn looked different but had no way to tell whether the pricing was fair, whether the invasive weeds had actually been addressed, whether the mulch had been properly layered. They also had no prompt to review — the business assumed 'they know to review us.' Most customers, left with ambiguity, defaulted to silence rather than rating.
cases.what_worked: A short email sent the evening of each job: one photo of the before/after, a three-bullet 'here's what we did today,' and a single-question follow-up ('did anything look off when you walked it?'). A review request came three days later, after the customer had time to see the work in daylight. Review volume tripled in 90 days and the average rating rose from 4.2 to 4.7. The intervention didn't change the work; it changed the moment after the work where the customer sat alone with a job they couldn't evaluate.
cases.what_wasted_money: A year of 'please review us' cards left in mailboxes that produced almost no reviews. The card was addressing a moment too late — by the time the customer found the card in the kitchen a week later, the memory of the specific job had blurred into a general 'it was fine.'
cases.one_line_lesson: The moment the crew leaves, the customer is alone with ambiguity. If nothing from the business helps them interpret what they just received, the review they would have given becomes a review they never wrote.

cases.challenge_pattern: first-30-seconds mismatch — the experience promised by marketing (intimate neighborhood spot, warm welcome) breaks in the first half-minute because the entry experience is cold
cases.observation: Neighborhood Italian restaurant with strong Instagram presence — warm photos of the owner in the kitchen, family recipes, 'come in like you're visiting nonna's house.' Yelp reviews were quietly deflating over a year: 'good food, felt weird when we walked in,' 'food was great, never got greeted,' 'nobody seemed to see us.' The owner couldn't understand because she personally greeted every guest when she was on the floor. The gap: she was only on the floor 3-4 nights per week, and on other nights, no one had been assigned the greeting role. The front-of-house staff stayed at the back of the dining room at the POS. New guests walked in, stood in the doorway for 30-60 seconds, and felt invisible. By the time someone seated them, they were already primed to notice whatever went wrong next.
cases.what_worked: A formalized greeter role assigned to whoever was nearest the door — not a hostess station, just a responsibility: acknowledge the guest within ten seconds of entry, even if seating would take five minutes. A visible bar counter with a 'wait here, we'll seat you shortly' sign was added. The average star rating on reviews mentioning entrance experience recovered within six weeks. The kitchen didn't change. The owner's Instagram voice didn't change. The first 30 seconds finally matched.
cases.what_wasted_money: Six months of new menu items and an expensive social media photographer. The food and the brand weren't the problem; the entry moment was silently breaking the promise the brand made.
cases.one_line_lesson: Marketing builds the expectation; the first 30 seconds either confirms it or breaks it. A warm Instagram voice and a cold doorway is a promise the business is failing before anyone orders.

cases.challenge_pattern: failure-mode interaction — the specific moment when something goes wrong reveals whether the business is actually competent or just seems like it
cases.observation: Mid-priced restaurant group with three locations and strong service overall. One location had a pattern of negative reviews the others didn't — all mentioning the same moment: the point when a diner mentioned a food allergy or dietary restriction. At the other two locations, servers paused, confirmed they'd tell the kitchen directly, and often brought a modified dish with an explanation from the chef. At the problem location, the server took the note, disappeared, and sometimes the right thing arrived and sometimes it didn't — and when it didn't, there was no acknowledgment, no adjustment. Reviewers' language was specific: 'I told them I was gluten-free and they served me pasta without a word,' 'server seemed annoyed when I asked about dairy.' The moment of vulnerability — a guest naming a health constraint — was being handled as an inconvenience at one location and as a moment of trust at the others.
cases.what_worked: Explicit training for the problem location's staff on two micro-behaviors: (1) repeat the restriction back to the guest verbatim ('so no dairy, got it — I'll make sure the kitchen knows personally'); (2) when the dish arrives, confirm ('this one's dairy-free'). The behaviors cost zero dollars. Negative reviews mentioning allergies or restrictions effectively stopped within two months. The moment hadn't been a kitchen problem; it was a server-communication problem at the point when the guest was most anxious.
cases.what_wasted_money: A quarter of menu redesign and a new POS system rolled out to the problem location under the assumption the issue was kitchen execution. It was front-of-house handling at a specific moment that had been invisible to management.
cases.one_line_lesson: How a business handles the moment a customer names a vulnerability — an allergy, a concern, a problem — is where trust is actually built or broken. Smooth routine service is table stakes; the failure-mode interaction is the real test.

cases.challenge_pattern: first-class-to-second-class drop — the gap between a trial class and a returning class where the customer goes from 'being welcomed' to 'being a stranger again'
cases.observation: Boutique barre studio with a strong intro offer (first class free) and poor conversion from first-class-to-membership. Owner believed conversion was a pricing problem. The gap was actually at the second visit. The first visit was highly supported — a member of staff greeted the new person by name (pulled from the signup form), the instructor introduced them to the class, and the studio felt personal. The second visit, if the person returned, was invisible. No one remembered them, no one asked how the first class had been, they scanned in at the front desk like any regular. The experience dropped from 'welcome' to 'transaction' in one visit. Most new members who didn't return after the free class had said nothing; they simply felt the warmth was a sales technique and the real experience was colder.
cases.what_worked: A 15-minute process for reception each morning: pull the list of new-from-the-previous-day-or-two attendees, greet them by name when they walked in for their second visit, and ask one specific question about the first class ('did the instructor's pace work for you?'). The second visit conversion rate rose meaningfully. The studio didn't change pricing. It made the second visit feel like a continuation of the first rather than a relapse to anonymity.
cases.what_wasted_money: A year of offering a discounted second-class voucher to first-time attendees. The discount didn't address the gap — the question wasn't the price of the second class, it was whether the second class felt like a reason to come back.
cases.one_line_lesson: The first visit is marketing; the second visit is the test. If the warmth of the first visit doesn't continue into the second, the customer interprets the warmth as tactical — and won't come back to be sold to again.

cases.challenge_pattern: vulnerability moment — the specific point when a new member names an injury or limitation and the business either acknowledges it or pretends it didn't hear
cases.observation: Solo personal trainer with a waiting list who was losing 20% of new clients within the first month. The pattern was specific: new clients who mentioned an existing injury or physical concern during the first session — a knee, a back, a shoulder — were the ones who left. Clients who didn't mention anything stayed. The trainer assumed the injury-mentioners were self-selecting for 'not ready to train.' The gap was different: the trainer was acknowledging the injury verbally but not modifying the first workout accordingly — it was the same workout regardless. Clients who had named their injury and then watched themselves be put through exercises that aggravated it concluded the trainer hadn't listened, or worse, didn't know how to adapt. They didn't leave in anger; they simply didn't rebook.
cases.what_worked: A five-minute pre-workout review when any injury was mentioned: name the specific exercise modifications for the session, walk the client through what they'd be skipping and what they'd be doing instead, and at the end of the workout, confirm how the modified exercises had felt. Retention through month two rose sharply. The change wasn't in the training; it was in making the modification visible so the client knew they'd been heard.
cases.what_wasted_money: Three months of the trainer adding 'assessment intake forms' to the onboarding process. The forms captured the injuries; they didn't close the gap, because the gap wasn't information capture — it was the client's visible experience of being heard during the first workout.
cases.one_line_lesson: When a customer names a vulnerability, they're watching what you do next more than they're listening to what you say. Acknowledgment without visible adaptation reads as having been ignored — and the customer leaves without telling you why.

cases.challenge_pattern: post-purchase confirmation gap — the days between order confirmation and shipping notification where the customer is wondering whether the order actually went through
cases.observation: Small-batch skincare brand with a 24-48 hour handmade-to-order production window before shipping. Customers received a standard automated order confirmation immediately, then heard nothing for 2-3 days until a shipping notification arrived. The owner couldn't understand why first-time customer support requests were running high — 85% of them were 'did my order go through?' or 'is this still being processed?' The customers weren't anxious people; they were reacting to a 48-hour silence that felt, in e-commerce terms, wrong. They compared the experience to Amazon (ships in hours) rather than to a small handmade brand (takes time), because nothing in the silence told them they were getting a different kind of experience.
cases.what_worked: One additional email 18-24 hours after the order: a photo of the product being made (owner's hand pouring the product, labels drying, packaging being hand-folded), with two sentences naming the person making it and when it would ship. Customer support tickets on 'is this still processing' dropped by about 80%. Conversion rate on repeat purchases rose in the same period. The email wasn't logistics information — it was reframing the silence as craft instead of absence.
cases.what_wasted_money: Six months of customer-service staffing at a level sized for the inquiry volume. Half the inquiry volume was self-generated by an experience that had left a silence the brand hadn't filled.
cases.one_line_lesson: In an e-commerce category trained by Amazon, 48 hours of silence after purchase reads as a problem — unless something in the silence tells the customer they're getting a different experience, not a worse one.

cases.challenge_pattern: unboxing mismatch — the moment the package arrives and the physical experience doesn't match the brand experience the customer had online
cases.observation: Premium candle brand ($38+ per candle) with careful Instagram imagery, thoughtful product photography, and craft-focused marketing. Conversion was strong; repeat purchase rate was below category norm. The gap was the unboxing: candles arrived in plain brown cardboard with a basic packing slip, minimal internal packaging, and — for some customers — a visibly scuffed box. The brand experience online was luxe; the brand experience in hand was Amazon generic. First-time buyers, especially gift-givers, received the package and thought 'this is the same box my dog food comes in.' The candle itself was beautiful; the moment of arrival had reframed it as less than it was.
cases.what_worked: A branded internal wrap (tissue paper with a printed pattern, a simple card with the scent name and burn instructions written in the brand voice), a kraft-paper outer wrap around the candle box itself, and a stamp on the shipping box that read 'Handmade in Vermont.' Material cost per order rose $1.20. Repeat purchase rate rose notably in the next quarter, and organic Instagram unboxing posts from customers began appearing for the first time. The product hadn't changed; the moment of receiving had started matching the moment of choosing.
cases.what_wasted_money: A year of additional Meta spend trying to move repeat-purchase numbers. The spend was addressing the top of the funnel; the leak was at the package opening.
cases.one_line_lesson: In a premium DTC category, the unboxing is the moment the brand promise is either delivered or broken. Generic packaging on a premium product tells the customer they were the mark, not the audience.
